Advanced Web Designing.summary: Covers advanced HTML5 features, CSS, forms, and semantic tags.
Advanced Web Designing.detailedSummary:
Imagine building a super cool digital house on the internet! Advanced Web Designing teaches you how to build this house using HTML5, which acts like the bricks, and CSS, which acts like the paint and decorations [1, 2].
 
 **1. Smart Forms for Collecting Information**
 Think of a form like a school worksheet where you fill in your details [1, 3]. HTML5 gives us special, smart boxes to type in [3]. Instead of just plain text, you get special boxes designed specifically for picking colors, dates (like your birthday), emails, numbers, and even a search box [3-6]. You can also set rules, like making a box "required" so a user cannot skip it, or adding a "placeholder" which is a light, temporary text that gives a hint on what you need to type [7].
 
 **2. The Secret `<meta>` Tag**
 This tag is like a secret whisper to search engines like Google [8]. It tells the computer what your webpage is about, who wrote it, and gives it important keywords, but normal visitors won't see this secret message on their screen [8-10].
 
 **3. CSS (Cascading Style Sheets) - The Magic Paintbrush**
 If HTML builds the walls, CSS is the magic paintbrush that makes the website look beautiful [2].
 * **Styles:** You can use it to change text colors, make the font bigger, add colorful borders, or put a beautiful background picture behind your text [11-14].
 * **Types of Paintbrushes:** There are three ways to style: *Inline* (decorating just one specific word or line), *Internal* (decorating one whole page), and *External* (using one main rulebook to decorate the entire website all at once) [15].
 * **Positioning:** CSS lets you move things around exactly where you want them, like pinning a block of text to the top of the screen or floating a picture to the left or right side [16-18].
 
 **4. Semantic Tags (Giving Things Proper Names)**
 In the past, website builders called every section a generic "box." Now, HTML5 uses special names so the computer understands exactly what part of the page it is looking at [19]. For example:
 * `<header>` is used for the very top title area [19].
 * `<nav>` is used for the menu links to navigate around the site [20].
 * `<article>` is used for the main story or news [21].
 * `<footer>` is used for the very bottom of the page [21].
 
 **5. Lists, Music, and Movies**
 * **Lists:** You can organize information neatly using Numbered lists (1, 2, 3...), Bulleted lists (using little dots), or even put a smaller list inside a bigger list (Nested list) [21-23].
 * **Media:** You can easily add your favorite music using the `<audio>` tag and movies using the `<video>` tag directly into your webpage so your friends can click "play" and watch [24-27].
 
 **6. Image Maps and Iframes**
 * **Image Map:** Imagine a single picture of a world map. With an image map, you can make it so clicking on different countries in that one picture takes you to completely different web pages [28, 29].
 * **Iframe:** An Iframe is like cutting a little square window in your webpage to peek at another completely different webpage inside it [30].
 
 **7. Web Hosting**
 Finally, once your digital house is perfectly built on your computer, you need to put it on the internet so everyone in the world can visit it. Renting a space on the internet to store your website is called Web Hosting [31, 32].

Introduction to SEO.summary: Focuses on search engine optimization basics to improve website rankings.
Introduction to SEO.detailedSummary:
Imagine the internet is a giant library with millions of books, which are like websites [1]. When you want to find something, you ask an online program called a Search Engine, like Google, Yahoo, or Bing [1]. SEO stands for Search Engine Optimization [2, 3]. It is a special way of designing your website so that the Search Engine likes it best and puts it at the very top of the search results [2, 3]. When your website is at the top, many more people will visit it [2, 3].
 
 There are two main areas where we can do this optimization:
 1. On-Page SEO (Inside your website): This is all the work you do directly on your own website [1, 2]. It involves writing really good information, giving your pages clear titles, and putting your words in the right places [2]. It also means keeping your website's computer code neat and organized [4]. 
 2. Off-Page SEO (Outside your website): This is about making your website famous across the internet [1, 2]. It focuses on building relationships with other websites, so they share your links and talk about you [1, 2].
 
 When trying to be at the top, there are two ways to play the game:
 * White Hat SEO (The Good Way): These are the good techniques that search engines want you to use [3, 5]. It means using good spelling, writing real and helpful sentences, and labeling your pictures properly [4, 5].
 * Black Hat SEO (The Cheating Way): These are bad tricks that search engines do not approve of [5]. People might copy other websites, hide invisible text, or use fake links to trick the computer [5].
 
 Here are some of the most important tricks to improve a website:
 * Page Titles and Descriptions: Every page needs a catchy and clear title so the search engine knows exactly what the page is about [6]. You also write a short "Meta-description" which is a tiny summary of the page to help the search engine understand it better [6].
 * Keywords: These are the exact words people type into the search bar when looking for something [7]. Picking the right keywords and putting them in your website helps the search engine match your website to the person searching [7].
 * Image Tags (Alt attributes): Computers cannot "see" pictures the way human eyes do [6]. So, we add a hidden description to the image called an "Alt attribute" so the search engine understands what the picture shows even if it cannot see it [6].
 * Backlinks: A backlink is when another website puts a link on their page that brings people to your website [7]. Search engines see this as a "vote of confidence," meaning if others link to you, your website must be really good [7].
 * Social Media and Bookmarking: Saving your website links (bookmarking) and sharing your website on places like Facebook and Twitter helps build a community and makes your website more trusted [8-10].
 * Speed and Mobile-Friendliness: Your website must load very quickly and look perfectly fine on a mobile phone, because search engines want users to have a fast and easy time [11].
 
 Finally, just like a person goes to a doctor for a checkup, a website needs an "SEO Audit" [10]. An SEO Audit is a tool that checks the health of your website to see if it is fast, safe, and easy to use [10]. It gives you a report card and tells you exactly what you need to fix so your website can become a top-ranked champion [10, 12].

Advanced Javascript.summary: Explains JavaScript loops, statements, and the Document Object Model (DOM).
Advanced Javascript.detailedSummary:
JavaScript is a computer language that makes web pages come alive, and in Advanced JavaScript, we learn special tools to control exactly how a website behaves [1]. 
 
 **1. Making Choices (The Switch Case Statement)**
 Sometimes a computer program needs to make a choice from a long list of options. Imagine you have a box of different colored buttons, and you want a different music track to play depending on the color you push. A `switch` statement looks at a value and matches it with the right "case" or rule to follow [2]. It is a simple way for the computer to decide what to do next without getting confused [2].
 
 **2. Repeating Actions (Loops)**
 If you want to tell a computer to do the same thing 10 times, you don't need to write the same instruction 10 times. Instead, you use a "loop" to repeat it automatically [3].
 * **The 'for' loop:** This is like a counting machine [3]. You tell it exactly where to start (like starting at number 1), when to stop (like stopping at number 5), and how to count [4].
 * **The 'while' loop:** This tells the computer to keep repeating an action *while* a certain rule is true [5]. For example, it is like saying, "Keep clapping your hands *while* the music is playing" [5].
 * **Break and Continue:** Sometimes you need to stop a loop early. The `break` statement is like an emergency exit button that immediately jumps you out of the loop [6]. The `continue` statement is like skipping your turn in a board game; it skips the current action and jumps right to the next round [6].
 
 **3. The Document Object Model (DOM)**
 Imagine a web page is a big house built out of Lego blocks. When your web browser opens a page, it creates a special blueprint of that house called the Document Object Model, or DOM [7]. The DOM organizes every single piece of the webpage (like headings, paragraphs, and images) into a structure [7, 8]. Because of the DOM, JavaScript can act like a builder who reads the blueprint to find specific Lego pieces and change them dynamically [8]. For example, JavaScript can use a tool called `innerHTML` to magically swap out the words on your screen right after you click a button [9].

Emerging Technologies.summary: Introduces IOT, Cloud computing, Artificial Intelligence (AI), and 5G.
Emerging Technologies.detailedSummary:
Imagine if the everyday objects around you could talk to each other over the internet! That is exactly what the **Internet of Things (IoT)** does [1]. It connects normal things like cars, home appliances, and cameras to the internet so they can collect and share information [1, 2]. For example, IoT gives us smart lights that turn on automatically, smart thermostats that control the temperature of a house, and smart cameras that keep homes safe [3]. This helps save a lot of time and human effort, but it can also be very complicated to build and might share too much of our private information [2, 3].
 
 Next, think of a giant, invisible backpack in the sky where you can store all your games, pictures, and files. This is called **Cloud Computing**! Instead of keeping all your information on one single computer, you keep it on the internet so you can access it from anywhere in the world [4, 5]. Companies use the cloud to rent out storage space, building tools, or even software programs so they do not have to buy expensive computer parts themselves [4, 6, 7]. It saves a lot of money and keeps data very safe [5, 7].
 
 Have you ever talked to a voice assistant on a phone, like Siri or Google, and it answered your question? That is **Artificial Intelligence (AI)** [8]. AI is all about creating smart machines that can think, learn, and react just like human beings [5]. With AI, computers can learn to recognize images, understand human language, and make decisions very quickly [9, 10]. It is amazing because machines do not make the same silly mistakes that humans do [10]. However, creating these smart machines costs a lot of money, and some people worry that robots might end up taking over human jobs [11].
 
 Finally, **5G** is the newest and fastest invisible highway for mobile phones and internet [11]. It is the fifth generation of wireless technology and is many times faster than the 4G we use today [11]. Because it is so incredibly fast, 5G lets us play online games smoothly, watch ultra-high-definition videos without waiting, and even helps run cars that drive themselves and virtual classrooms [12, 13]. It is the super-speed technology that connects all these other amazing future inventions together [12]!

Server-Side Scripting (PHP).summary: Covers PHP programming for dynamic web pages and database connectivity.
Server-Side Scripting (PHP).detailedSummary:
**Server-Side Scripting and PHP**
 Imagine a restaurant where you are the customer (the web browser) and the kitchen is the "server" [1]. PHP is a computer language that acts like the chef in the kitchen [2]. It prepares the web page in the background and sends the finished page to your screen [1, 2]. PHP stands for Hypertext Preprocessor [3]. It is free to use, very simple to learn, and works on almost all computers [4, 5]. 
 
 **Writing PHP Code**
 When you write a PHP program, you put the secret instructions between special tags that look like `<?php` and `?>` [6]. These files are always saved with a `.php` ending [6]. If you want the computer to display words like "Hello World" on the screen, you use a simple command called `echo` [6]. 
 
 **Variables (Magic Boxes)**
 A variable is like a magic box where you can store information to use later [7]. In PHP, the name of every magic box must start with a dollar sign `$`, like `$name` or `$age` [7]. These boxes can hold different types of data, such as words (Strings), whole numbers (Integers), numbers with decimal dots (Floats), or just True and False (Booleans) [8]. 
 
 **Decisions and Loops**
 Sometimes we want the computer to make a choice based on a rule, so we use "if-else" statements [9]. For example, "If your score is more than 60, you pass; else, try again" [9]. Other times, we want the computer to repeat a task over and over [10]. We use "Loops" for this, which tell the computer to keep doing the same task until a specific condition is met [10, 11]. 
 
 **Arrays (Big Shelves)**
 If you have many things to store, instead of making a new magic box for each item, you can use an "Array" [12]. An array is like a big shelf with many drawers [12]. You can put multiple values inside this single shelf and find them easily by using a number or a special name to open the right drawer [12-14].
 
 **Functions (Recipes)**
 A function is a block of code that acts exactly like a recipe [14, 15]. Once you write the steps down, you can use that same recipe over and over in your program without having to rewrite the steps [14]. PHP comes with many ready-made recipes to count or reverse words, but you can also create your very own custom recipes [14-16].
 
 **Handling Forms**
 When you fill out a form on a website, like typing your name and password, PHP collects that information using two main methods called GET and POST [17-19]. POST is like a hidden, sealed envelope, so it is perfect and safe for hiding secret passwords [20]. GET is like a postcard where everyone can see your information right in the web address link, so it should never be used for secrets [20].
 
 **Connecting to a Database**
 A database is a giant digital filing cabinet where a website securely stores big lists of information, like student records or game scores [21]. PHP uses a special connection tool called PDO (PHP Data Object) to talk to this big filing cabinet [22]. This allows the website to add new records into the cabinet or fetch old records to show them to you on the screen safely [21-23].
 
 **Cookies and Sessions**
 A "cookie" is a tiny text file a website leaves on your computer to remember who you are, just like giving you a small name tag [24]. A "session" is a memory saved on the server's side that remembers what you are doing while you click around, like keeping you safely logged into your email account while you visit different pages [24].

E-Commerce and E-Governance.summary: Explains online buying/selling, trade cycles, payment modes, and e-governance types.
E-Commerce and E-Governance.detailedSummary:
**E-Commerce (Electronic Commerce)**
 Imagine you want to buy a toy or a book. Instead of walking to a physical store, you use a computer or a mobile phone to buy it over the internet. This process of buying and selling things online is called E-Commerce [1]. It is like a magic shop that never closesÃ¢â¬âit is open 24 hours a day, 7 days a week, and you can buy things from anywhere in the whole world without leaving your room [2-4]. 
 
 **How is E-Commerce different from Normal Shopping?**
 In normal shopping (Traditional Commerce), you talk to a shopkeeper face-to-face, pay with paper money, and get your toy right away [3, 5]. In E-Commerce, you look at a screen instead of a face, pay using digital money (like cards or apps), and wait a little while for the item to be delivered to your house [2, 3]. 
 
 **Types of E-Commerce:**
 There are different ways people and businesses trade online [6]:
 1. **B2C (Business to Consumer):** A big company sells directly to a regular person (a consumer). For example, your parents buying shoes for you from Amazon or Flipkart [4].
 2. **B2B (Business to Business):** One company sells things to another company, like a factory buying parts from another factory [7].
 3. **C2C (Consumer to Consumer):** Regular people selling things to other regular people. For example, if you want to sell your old bicycle, you can put it on a website like OLX for another person to buy [6].
 4. **C2B (Consumer to Business):** A regular person offering a product or service to a business, like someone writing a blog to help a company sell its items [8].
 
 **The Trade Cycle (The Steps of Buying Online):**
 Every time you buy something online, you complete a "trade cycle," which has four simple steps [9]:
 1. **Pre-Sales:** You search the internet for the toy you want and find the best price [9].
 2. **Execution:** You place the order, and the seller gets it ready and delivers it to you [9].
 3. **Settlement:** You receive a bill (invoice) and pay the money [10].
 4. **After-Sales:** This is the help you get after buying. If the toy is broken, you can complain or use the warranty to get it fixed [10].
 
 **How to Pay Online (Payment Modes):**
 Since you cannot hand paper money through a computer screen, people use electronic payments [11]:
 * **Credit Cards:** Plastic cards used to pay online, which is the most common way [12].
 * **Mobile Payments:** Using special apps on a smartphone, like Google Pay, Paytm, or BHIM, where you can just scan a code [12].
 * **Bank Transfers:** Sending money safely from one bank account directly to another bank account [13].
 * **E-Wallets:** A digital purse on your phone where you can store money for future online shopping [13].
 
 **E-Governance (Electronic Governance)**
 Just like we use the internet for shopping, the government uses the internet to help citizens and run the country smoothly. This is called E-Governance [14]. Its goal is to make government work "SMART," which stands for Simple, Moral, Accessible, Responsive, and Transparent [15]. 
 
 **Types of E-Governance:**
 1. **G2C (Government to Citizen):** The government helps regular people online. For example, letting citizens pay their taxes or renew their driving licenses from their computers at home [16].
 2. **G2B (Government to Business):** The government helps businesses save time by doing their paperwork and sharing forms online [16, 17].
 3. **G2G (Government to Government):** Different government offices and departments use computers to talk to each other and share information safely [17].
 4. **G2E (Government to Employee):** The government helps its own workers. Employees can go online to apply for holidays, check their salary, or get training [18].
 
 **Keeping Things Safe (Security Measures):**
 Since so much important information and money travel through the internet, we have to keep it very safe from bad guys [19]:
 * **Encryption:** This is like writing in a secret code. It takes your readable information (like passwords) and scrambles it into a coded text so nobody else can read it. When it reaches the right person, it gets unscrambled [19].
 * **Digital Signatures:** These are like electronic fingerprints on a document. They prove that a message or document is real and actually came from you [20].
 * **Digital Certificates:** These act like an electronic ID card or "password." They prove that a person or a company is authorized and safe to do business with [21].

Introduction To Corporate Finance.summary: Details the meaning, importance, fixed, and working capital requirements.
Introduction To Corporate Finance.detailedSummary:
**Introduction to Corporate Finance**
 
 Imagine a business is like a giant car. To make the car run, it needs fuel. In the business world, that fuel is called "finance" or money [1]. Corporate finance is all about how a company gets this money and how it carefully uses it to grow [1]. The person in charge of this money has to make two very important choices:
 1. **The Financing Decision:** This is deciding where to get the money from [2]. It is just like deciding whether to use your piggy bank money or ask your parents for a loan to buy a lemonade stand. 
 2. **The Investment Decision:** Once the company has the money, they have to decide exactly how to spend it so they can make even more money [3]. 
 
 **Why is Corporate Finance Important?**
 Managing this money is super important for the business to stay alive and healthy. Here is why:
 * **Making Good Choices:** It helps the boss decide which ideas will make the most profit [4].
 * **Starting New Things:** Whenever a business wants to start a brand new project, it needs money to do it [5].
 * **Inventing and Improving:** It pays for researching new ideas and making products better and cooler for customers [5].
 * **Everyday Smooth Running:** It ensures there is always cash to pay the workers their salaries on time and buy supplies [6].
 * **Growing Bigger:** If the company wants to build more factories or sell different items, finance provides the money to expand [7].
 * **Safety Net for Risks:** If something bad happens, like a natural disaster or a sudden drop in sales, having money helps the company survive [7].
 * **Buying New Machines:** When old machines break down or get too old, the company uses this money to replace them [8].
 * **Paying Fees:** It provides the cash needed to pay taxes to the government [8].
 
 **Fixed Capital Requirements**
 When you first open a business, you need to buy big, heavy things that you will keep for a very long time, like the land, the factory building, and large machines [9]. The money used to buy these permanent things is called **Fixed Capital** [9]. You do not buy these things to sell them to customers; you use them to build your products [9]. A company might need a lot or a little bit of Fixed Capital depending on:
 * **What the business does:** A giant car factory needs to buy massive, expensive machines, while a small grocery shop does not need as much [10].
 * **How big the business is:** A very large business needs more money for bigger buildings [10].
 * **Renting vs. Buying:** If the business owner decides to just rent a building instead of buying it, they will not need as much fixed capital [11].
 * **Buying Old Items:** If the business buys used, older machines instead of brand new ones, it costs less money [12].
 
 **Working Capital Requirements**
 Even after buying the big buildings and machines, the business still needs money every single day [13]. They need cash to buy the raw materials (like buying lemons and sugar for a lemonade stand), to pay the electricity bills, and to cover daily expenses [13]. The money used to run the business day-to-day is called **Working Capital** [13]. A company's need for Working Capital changes based on:
 * **The type of items sold:** If a business sells everyday items like bread and milk, cash comes in quickly every day, so they need less working capital [14]. If they sell expensive luxury items like jewelry, sales are slower, so they need more cash on hand to survive while waiting for a sale [14].
 * **The size of the business:** A company making millions of toys needs more daily cash than a company making just ten toys [15, 16].
 * **How long it takes to make the product:** If a product takes many weeks to build, the company's money is tied up for a long time, so they need more working capital [17].
 * **Good or Bad Times:** When the economy is doing great and people are buying a lot (a boom), the company needs more working capital to make extra products [17].

Sources of Corporate Finance.summary: Explains owned and borrowed capital like shares, debentures, and retained earnings.
Sources of Corporate Finance.detailedSummary:
Imagine a company is like a giant lemonade stand. To build it, buy lemons, and pay workers, the company needs money, which is called "finance" or "capital" [1, 2]. A company can collect this money in two main ways: using its own money (Owned Capital) or borrowing from others (Borrowed Capital) [3, 4]. 
 
 **1. Owned Capital (The Company's Own Money)**
 This is money provided by the owners of the company, and it stays in the business almost permanently [4].
 * **Shares:** Imagine the company's total money is a big pizza. The pizza is sliced into tiny pieces, and each piece is called a "share" [5]. People who buy these pieces become part-owners, or shareholders [5]. There are two main kinds of shares:
  * **Equity Shares:** These are the "brave" owners who take the most risk [6]. If the company makes a lot of money, they get a big reward, but if the company's income drops, their reward drops too [6]. 
  * **Preference Shares:** These are the "safe" owners. They are promised a fixed, guaranteed reward (called a dividend) and get paid before the equity owners do [7, 8].
 * **Retained Earnings:** Imagine you earn an allowance and save a portion in your piggy bank instead of spending it all. When a company makes a profit, it saves a part of it for the future instead of giving it all to the owners [8, 9]. This saved, accumulated profit is called retained earnings [8, 9].
 
 **2. Borrowed Capital (Borrowed Money)**
 Sometimes, a company's own money is not enough to do everything it wants, so it has to borrow money from outsiders [10]. The company must return this money later and must pay an extra fee called "interest" [11].
 * **Debentures and Bonds:** When a company needs a big loan, it can ask the public for money. In return, the company gives them a special certificate called a "debenture" or a "bond," which is a written promise that the company owes them money and will pay it back on a specific date with fixed interest [7, 12, 13]. 
 * **Public Deposits:** This is when the company borrows money from regular people for a shorter amount of time, usually between 6 months to 36 months [14].
 * **Bank Loans:** Just like regular people, companies can go to commercial banks and ask for loans to help pay for their business needs [15, 16].
 
 By mixing together owned money (like shares and savings) and borrowed money (like loans and debentures), a company builds what is called its "capital structure" so it can run smoothly and grow [3, 17].

Issue of Shares.summary: Covers the provisions and procedures related to the issue of shares.
Issue of Shares.detailedSummary:
**What is a Share?**
 Imagine a company is like a giant pizza. To build a big company, it needs a lot of money [1]. So, the company divides its total capital (the pizza) into many tiny, equal pieces. These tiny pieces are called "shares" [2]. When people buy these pieces with their money, they become "shareholders," which means they are part-owners of the company [2].
 
 **Types of Share Capital (The Pizza Sizes)**
 * **Authorised Capital:** This is the absolute maximum amount of money the company is legally allowed to raise by selling shares [3]. 
 * **Issued Capital:** The company might not want to sell all its shares at once. The portion of shares they are offering to sell right now is the issued capital [4].
 * **Subscribed Capital:** This is the number of shares that people actually agreed to buy [5].
 * **Called-up & Paid-up Capital:** The company usually doesn't ask for all the money at once; they let people pay in installments [6, 7]. "Called-up" is the money the company is asking for right now, and "Paid-up" is the money the people have actually given to the company [6, 7].
 
 **How Do Companies Sell Shares? (Methods of Issue)**
 * **To the Public (Public Offer):** The company invites everyone to buy shares [8].
  * *Initial Public Offer (IPO):* When a company offers its shares to the public for the very first time [9].
  * *Further Public Offer (FPO):* When a company that is already selling shares wants to sell even more to the public later [10].
  * The company can sell these shares at a "Fixed Price" (the price is set early) [11] or through "Book Building" (where people bid on a price range and the final price is decided later based on demand) [12].
 * **To Existing Owners:**
  * *Rights Issue:* The company asks the people who already own shares if they want to buy more first, before asking strangers [13].
  * *Bonus Issue:* The company gives completely free extra shares to its existing owners as a gift because the company made good profits [14].
 * **To Employees:** The company lets its own workers buy shares at a cheaper, discounted price as a reward for their hard work (through schemes like ESOS or ESPS) [15, 16].
 * **Private Placement:** Instead of asking the whole world, the company secretly sells shares to a small, specially chosen group of people [17].
 
 **Giving Out the Shares (Allotment)**
 When people want to buy shares, they send an application form and a little bit of money [18]. When the company's directors accept the application and officially give them the shares, it is called the "Allotment of Shares" [18, 19]. 
 
 **Share Certificate (The Ownership Ticket)**
 After getting the shares, the company gives the owner a special official paper called a "Share Certificate" [20]. It is like a receipt or an ID card that proves you are the real owner of those specific shares [20].
 
 **Asking for the Rest of the Money (Calls on Shares)**
 Since the company allows people to pay in installments, they will send a notice later asking for the remaining balance [21]. This demand for the rest of the unpaid money is known as a "Call" [21].
 
 **Taking the Shares Back (Forfeiture)**
 If someone buys a share but refuses to pay the remaining balance when the company "calls" for it, the company will cancel their ownership and take the shares back as a punishment [22]. This is called "Forfeiture" [22]. The person also loses the money they already paid [22].
 
 **Passing Shares to Others (Transfer & Transmission)**
 * **Transfer:** If you want to sell your share to a friend or give it as a gift, you can voluntarily pass it to them [23].
 * **Transmission:** If an owner sadly passes away or becomes medically unfit, the law automatically passes their shares safely to their family members or legal heirs [24].

Issue of Debentures.summary: Covers the provisions and procedures for issuing debentures to raise capital.
Issue of Debentures.detailedSummary:
**What is a Debenture?**
 Imagine a company needs a lot of money to grow, so it asks regular people for a long-term loan [1, 2]. When a person lends money to the company, the company gives them a special paper called a "Debenture Certificate" as proof [3]. This paper is a promise saying the company owes them money and will pay it back [3]. Until the money is returned, the company gives the lenders extra money called "interest" at a fixed rate, even if the company does not make a profit [4]. The people who give this loan are called creditors, not owners [5]. 
 
 **Important Rules the Company Must Follow:**
 To make sure the company keeps its promises and keeps the people's money safe, there are strict rules it must follow [6, 7]:
 * **No Voting:** Because these people are just lending money, they are not owners and cannot vote on company decisions [5, 8].
 * **A Guarantee:** The company must put up some of its property as a guarantee, which is called creating a "charge on assets" [9, 10]. If the company cannot pay the loan back, the lenders can sell that property to get their money [9].
 * **Time Limit:** The company usually has to return the money within 10 years [11].
 * **A Special Savings Account:** The company must create a special piggy bank called a "Debenture Redemption Reserve" [12]. They put some of their profit into this account every year to make sure they have enough cash saved up to pay everyone back [12, 13].
 * **A Protector:** If the company asks for loans from more than 500 people, it must hire a "Debenture Trustee" [12, 14]. A Trustee is like a superhero guard who protects the lenders and makes sure the company does not cheat them [12, 15].
 * **A Report Card:** The company must get a score called a "Credit Rating" from experts [16]. This score shows people if the company is safe and trustworthy to lend money to [17].
 
 **Step-by-Step Process to Ask for the Loan:**
 1. **Meeting of Bosses:** The leaders of the company, called the Board of Directors, hold a meeting to decide exactly how much money they need to borrow [18, 19].
 2. **Tell the Government:** They send their official plan to the government office that keeps track of companies [20].
 3. **Get a Score:** They get their credit rating score to prove they are safe [20].
 4. **Send an Invitation:** They put out a special invitation called a "Prospectus" to ask the public to lend them money [21]. 
 5. **Collect the Money:** People who want to lend money put it into a separate, special bank account that the company opens just for this purpose [21].
 6. **Give the Certificates:** The bosses meet again to officially accept the money and then hand out the Debenture Certificates to everyone within 6 months [21, 22].
 7. **Keep a List:** Finally, the company writes down the names of everyone who lent them money in a special record book so they never forget who they owe [22].

Deposits.summary: Explains the rules for accepting public deposits as a short-term finance source.
Deposits.detailedSummary:
Imagine a big toy company needs some extra money for a short time to buy more materials to build toys. Instead of going to a big bank, they ask normal people like you and me, or their own friends, to lend them the money. This borrowed money is called a **Deposit** [1]. 
 
 Here are the simple rules the company must follow when asking for this money:
 
 * **Time Limit:** The company can keep the money for at least 6 months, but no longer than 36 months (which is 3 years) [2]. They can't keep it forever! They also cannot accept money that you might ask for back immediately [3].
 * **Who can they ask?** Small private companies can only ask their friends and owners (called members or directors) for money [4]. But very big, rich companies (called Eligible Public Companies) or Government companies can ask anyone in the general public to lend them money [2, 4, 5].
 * **The "Thank You" Extra Money (Interest):** When you lend the company your money, they don't just give the exact amount back. They give you a little bit of extra money as a "thank you." This is called interest [1, 6].
 * **The Proof Paper (Deposit Receipt):** When you give them your money, they must give you a special piece of paper within 21 days [7]. This paper is called a Deposit Receipt. It proves that you gave them the money, and it tells you exactly how much extra interest you will get and exactly what date they will give the money back to you [7].
 * **The Superhero Guard (Deposit Trustee):** To make sure the company doesn't cheat you, they must hire a special guard called a Deposit Trustee [8]. If the company refuses to pay you back, this Trustee steps in and fights to protect you and help you get your money back [8].
 * **Safety Net (Deposit Insurance):** The company also has to buy a special safety net called Deposit Insurance [9]. If something very bad happens and the company loses its money, this insurance makes sure you still get your money back (up to Rs. 20,000) [9].
 * **Report Card (Credit Rating):** Before you give them money, the company has to show a "report card" from a special agency [10]. This rating tells you if the company is good, strong, and safe to lend money to, or if they are bad at paying people back on time [10]. 
 * **Spreading the Word (Advertisements):** If the company wants to ask the public for money, they must put a big advertisement in the newspaper so everyone knows all the true details about it [11, 12].
 
 So, a public deposit is just a very safe, rule-filled way for a company to borrow your piggy-bank money for a short time and give it back to you later with a little extra bonus! [1].

Correspondence with Members.summary: Details the precautions and scenarios for drafting letters to shareholders.
Correspondence with Members.detailedSummary:
Imagine a company is like a big club, and the "members" or "shareholders" are the people who own a small piece of this club [1]. The "Company Secretary" is like the club's main messenger, whose job is to write letters to these members to share news and answer questions [1]. Writing these letters is a very important job, so the messenger must follow some special rules (called precautions) to make sure everyone is happy and understands the messages [2, 3].
 
 **Special Rules for Writing Letters (Precautions):**
 * **Always Tell the Truth (Correct Information):** The messenger must always give the exact and correct facts to the members [3].
 * **Use Easy Words (Lucid Language):** They should use simple words and short sentences so that anyone can understand, instead of using big, confusing words [3].
 * **Reply Fast (Prompt Response):** When a member asks a question or has a problem, the messenger needs to reply very quickly without making them wait [4].
 * **Keep Secrets Safe (Secrecy):** The messenger must be very good at keeping the club's private secrets safe and not sharing them in the letters [4].
 * **Be Very Nice (Politeness):** It is super important to always be polite, kind, and never use rude words, even if answering a complaint [4].
 * **Follow the Rules (Legal Matters):** The messenger has to follow all the strict laws of the government when writing [5].
 * **Care About Feelings (Consideration):** They must think about the feelings of the members, especially if they have to say "no" to something, making sure not to hurt their feelings [5].
 * **Make the Club Look Good (Image of the Company):** By being helpful and polite, the messenger makes the whole club look really good and friendly to everyone [6].
 
 **When Does the Messenger Send Letters? (Scenarios):**
 There are many different times when the messenger needs to send a letter to a member [6]. 
 * **Giving Proof of Ownership (Share Certificate):** They send a letter to give a member a special paper certificate that proves they officially own a piece of the club [6, 7].
 * **Sharing the Money Made (Payment of Dividend):** When the club makes money, the messenger sends a letter with a special check to give the members their share of the profits [6, 8, 9].
 * **Giving Free Gifts (Bonus Shares):** If the club decides to give out free extra pieces of the company as a gift, the messenger writes a letter to tell them the good news [6, 10, 11].
 * **Answering Worries (Replying to Queries):** Sometimes members are sad or confused, like if they got less money than they expected, so the messenger writes a gentle letter to explain why [12-14].
 * **Inviting to Meetings:** The messenger writes to invite all the members to big club meetings [6].
 * **Helping Pass on Pieces of the Club:** They write letters when a member wants to give their piece of the club to a friend or a family member [6, 12].

Correspondence with Debentureholders.summary: Outlines the procedures for writing letters regarding debenture allotment, interest, and redemption.
Correspondence with Debentureholders.detailedSummary:
Imagine a company needs to borrow money to do its work [1]. Sometimes, they borrow money from regular people instead of a bank, and these people are called "debentureholders" [1]. The company gives them a special paper called a "debenture" as a promise to pay the money back [1]. "Correspondence" is simply a big word for writing letters [2]. A person at the company called the Company Secretary has the job of writing letters to these lenders to keep them informed [2]. 
 
 When the Secretary writes these letters, they must follow some very important rules to be a good communicator [2, 3]:
 * **Politeness:** They must always use kind and respectful words, and never be rude [4].
 * **Quick Replies:** They must answer questions fast without making the lenders wait [4].
 * **Follow the Law:** They must obey all the government rules for companies [4].
 * **Honesty:** They need to tell the truth about how the company is doing [5].
 * **Keep it Short:** They should keep the letters brief and straight to the point without writing unnecessary words [5].
 * **Correct Information:** They must make sure all the details and numbers are exactly right [5].
 * **Keep Secrets:** They cannot share the company's private secrets in the letters [6].
 * **Good Image:** They should always make the company look good and friendly [6].
 
 The Secretary writes letters to the lenders for four main reasons [3, 6]:
 * **1. Allotment of Debentures:** This letter tells the person, "We received your money, and here is your official paper to prove you lent it to us!" [7].
 * **2. Payment of Interest:** Because the person lent the company money, the company pays them a little extra money as a "thank you," which is called "interest" [8]. This letter tells them that their extra money is being sent, either as a paper check in the mail or sent directly to their bank account using a computer [9].
 * **3. Conversion:** Sometimes, the lenders are given the choice to trade their loan paper to become actual part-owners of the company [10]. This letter explains that their debentures are being changed into "shares," which means they are now owners [10].
 * **4. Redemption:** When the borrowing time is finally over, the company has to give the original money back to the lender [11]. "Redemption" means returning the money [11]. This letter tells the lender that it is time to get their money back and asks them to fill out a special form to receive it [12].

Correspondence with Depositors.summary: Guides the secretary on corresponding about acceptance, renewal, and repayment of deposits.
Correspondence with Depositors.detailedSummary:
Imagine a company is like a giant piggy bank that sometimes needs to borrow extra money to buy things it needs for its business. It borrows this money for a short time, usually between 6 to 36 months [1]. The people who lend their savings to the company are called "Depositors" [1]. Because they lent their money, the company promises to give it all back on a specific date and regularly pays them a little extra reward money called "Interest" [1].
 
 The company has a special worker called a "Secretary" who is in charge of talking to these depositors [2]. The Secretary must write letters to them on behalf of the company's bosses (the Board of Directors) [2]. When writing these letters, the Secretary has to be very careful, polite, quick to reply, and must make sure all the information is completely true and follows the law [2-4]. 
 
 The Secretary mainly writes four types of letters to help the depositors understand what is happening with their money:
 
 **1. Acceptance of Deposits (Saying Thank You)**
 When a person first gives their money to the company, the Secretary writes a letter to say, "Thank you for trusting us and investing in our company!" [5]. Along with this letter, the Secretary sends a special paper called a "Deposit Receipt" within 21 days, which is a proof that the company safely received their money [5].
 
 **2. Payment of Interest (Sending the Reward)**
 While the company holds the money, it has to pay the depositors their promised extra reward (interest) [6]. The Secretary writes a letter to tell the depositor that their interest is ready [6, 7]. The company can send this reward money in two ways: either by mailing a special bank cheque called an "Interest Warrant," or by using a computer to send the money directly into the depositor's bank account (Electronic payment) [6, 7].
 
 **3. Renewal of Deposits (Staying a Little Longer)**
 Sometimes, when the borrowing time is almost up, the depositor says, "You can keep my money for another year or two!" [8]. This is called a "Renewal." The Secretary writes a letter to acknowledge this request, confirming that the money will stay with the company for a new time period, and sends the depositor a brand new Deposit Receipt [9].
 
 **4. Repayment of Deposits (Giving the Money Back)**
 When the agreed time is finally over and the money reaches its "maturity," the company must return it [10]. This is called "Repayment." The Secretary writes a letter telling the depositor that their time is up and securely sends their original money back, usually by attaching a crossed bank cheque [10, 11].

Depository System.summary: Explains the meaning, importance, and constituents (NSDL, CDSL) of the depository system.
Depository System.detailedSummary:
Imagine you have a lot of precious paper tickets, and if you lose them, you lose your money. Keeping paper tickets safe from getting torn, lost, or stolen is very hard [1]. The **Depository System** is a brilliant solution that solves this exact problem!
 
 **What is a Depository System?**
 It is exactly like a bank, but instead of keeping your money safe, it keeps your "securities" (like shares of a company) safe in a computer [2, 3]. Instead of holding physical paper certificates, everything is stored electronically, which is also called "dematerialized" or "demat" form [2, 4]. 
 
 **Why is it Important?**
 Before this computer system, people had to manage huge piles of paperwork, and sometimes paper certificates were stolen, damaged, or took a very long time to hand over to someone else [1, 5]. The Depository System is important because it makes keeping shares completely safe, removes the need for paperwork, and makes buying, selling, or transferring shares super fast and easy [5-7]. 
 
 **Who are the Main Helpers (Constituents) in this System?**
 There are four main members that make this system work together like a team [8]:
 1. **The Depository (The Big Safe):** This is the main organization that holds all the digital shares safely on their computers [8]. It acts just like the central bank for shares [8]. 
 2. **The Depository Participant or DP (The Agent):** You cannot talk directly to the Big Safe. You have to talk to their agent, known as the DP [8, 9]. A DP is usually a bank or a broker that helps you open your digital account (called a Demat account) and acts as the bridge between you and the Depository [9].
 3. **The Beneficial Owner or BO (The Investor):** This is you! This is the person who actually buys and owns the shares and gets all the benefits from them [10].
 4. **The Issuer Company:** This is the company that created and sold the shares to you in the first place [11].
 
 **What are NSDL and CDSL?**
 In India, we have two big depositories (Big Safes) that keep everyone's shares secure [12, 13]:
 * **NSDL (National Securities Depository Limited):** This is the very first and largest depository in India, which started in 1996 [13].
 * **CDSL (Central Depository Services Limited):** This is the second depository, which started working in 1999 [14].
 
 Both NSDL and CDSL do the exact same job: they make sure that when you buy a share, it stays perfectly safe in your digital account without any risk of getting lost or damaged [14, 15].

Dividend and Interest.summary: Covers provisions on sources, declaration, and payment of interim and final dividends.
Dividend and Interest.detailedSummary:
Imagine a company is like a big lemonade stand owned by many people (called shareholders). When the stand makes extra money, that extra money is called "profit." 
 
 **1. What is a Dividend?**
 A "Dividend" is simply a slice of that profit shared with the owners [1]. It is a reward for giving their money to help start the business. 
 
 **2. Two Types of Dividends**
 * **Interim Dividend:** Think of this as a mid-day snack. It is a portion of the profit given out in the *middle* of the year, before the company finishes counting all its money for the whole year [2]. The managers of the company (called the Board of Directors) decide to give this [2].
 * **Final Dividend:** Think of this as the big dessert after dinner. It is given at the *end* of the year after the company carefully counts all its profits [3]. The managers suggest how much to give, but all the owners have to vote and agree on it at a big yearly meeting [4]. 
 
 **3. Where Does the Dividend Money Come From? (Sources)**
 A company cannot just use any money to pay a dividend. They can only use real profits they made this year, profits they saved up from past years, or sometimes money given by the government for this purpose [5]. They are strictly forbidden from using the core money (capital) that is needed to keep the business running [5, 6].
 
 **4. How is it Announced? (Declaration)**
 When the managers decide they want to pay a dividend, they hold a special meeting [7]. Once they officially promise to pay a dividend, they cannot take that promise back [4]. To make sure the money is safe, the company must take the promised dividend money and put it into a completely separate bank account within 5 days [8]. 
 
 **5. How is the Money Given? (Payment)**
 * The company must pay the dividend using real money (like a check or a direct electronic transfer to a bank account) [9, 10]. They cannot pay by giving away gifts or items [6, 9]. 
 * They must pay it very quicklyÃ¢â¬âwithin 30 days of making the promise [11]. 
 * If an owner forgets to collect their dividend, the company puts it into a special "Unpaid" safety box [12]. If nobody claims that money for 7 whole years, it is sent to a special government fund that helps protect and educate investors [13].
 
 **6. What is Interest?**
 While a dividend is a share of profit for the *owners*, "Interest" is a fee paid to *lenders* [14]. If the company borrows money from someone, they have to pay a fixed extra charge as a "thank you" for letting them use the money [15]. The biggest difference is that a company only pays a dividend if they made a profit, but they *must* pay interest even if they lost money that year [16, 17].

Financial Market.summary: Discusses the classification and functions of money markets and capital markets.
Financial Market.detailedSummary:
Imagine you have extra pocket money saved up in your piggy bank, and your friend needs some money to build a lemonade stand. A **Financial Market** is exactly like a matching place where people who have extra money (investors) can lend it to people or businesses who need money (borrowers) [1]. Instead of trading toys, people trade "financial instruments," which are special legal papers with monetary value [2]. 
 
 **What does the Financial Market do?**
 * **Puts Money to Work:** Instead of letting money sit around doing nothing, it gives the money to businesses that will use it to create things and grow [3].
 * **Helps People Earn More:** When you lend your money out, you get extra money back as a reward, like interest or dividend [3].
 * **Easy Cash (Liquidity):** If you ever need your cash back quickly, the market lets you easily sell your financial papers to someone else [4].
 
 The Financial Market is divided into two main areas: the Money Market and the Capital Market [5].
 
 **1. The Money Market (Short-Term)**
 This market is used when people or businesses need to borrow or lend money for a very short time, ranging from just one single day up to one year [5], [6]. It is mostly used by big banks, the government, and large companies to meet their quick, everyday money needs [7]. The special papers they trade here have names like Treasury Bills, Commercial Papers, and Certificates of Deposit [6], [8]. Because the time is so short, it is very easy to turn these papers back into cash [6].
 
 **2. The Capital Market (Long-Term)**
 This market is used when businesses need a huge amount of money for a long time, which means more than one year [9], [10]. They use this money to build big things like new businesses or factories [11]. In this market, companies sell papers called Equity Shares, Preference Shares, Debentures, and Bonds [12]. 
 
 The Capital Market has two smaller parts:
 * **Primary Market (New Market):** This is where a company sells its brand-new shares to the public for the very first time to raise fresh money [13], [14].
 * **Secondary Market (Old Market):** Also known as the stock market, this is where people who already bought shares can sell them to other people [13], [15]. There are no fresh issues here; it is just people trading existing shares with each other [16].

Stock Exchange.summary: Covers the working of stock exchanges like BSE and NSE, and related terminology.
Stock Exchange.detailedSummary:
**What is a Stock Exchange?**
 Imagine a giant supermarket, but instead of buying toys or groceries, people buy and sell small parts of large companies [1]. A "Stock Exchange" is simply a specific, organized place where people trade these small parts, which are called shares or securities [2, 3]. It is also known as the Share Market [2]. You can only buy and sell shares that have already been issued by companies, making it a market for "second-hand" shares [4]. 
 
 **Why is it useful?**
 The stock exchange is a safe place because it has strict rules to protect people's money [5]. It helps people save money, helps companies gather money to grow, and allows investors to easily turn their shares back into cash whenever they need it [6-8]. The stock market also acts like a mirror that shows how healthy the country's overall economy is [5].
 
 **The Two Big Markets in India:**
 In India, there are two main places where this trading happens [9]:
 1. **BSE (Bombay Stock Exchange):** Started in 1875, this is the oldest share market in all of Asia [9]. It uses a computer system called BOLT for trading [10]. To check if the BSE is doing well, people look at a score called the "Sensex," which tracks the performance of the top 30 companies [11].
 2. **NSE (National Stock Exchange):** Started in 1992, this is the largest and most modern share market in India [10]. It was the first market in the country to use a fully automated electronic computer screen for trading [12]. Its score is called the "Nifty," which tracks 50 major companies [13].
 
 **Important Words to Know:**
 * **Broker:** An agent or helper who buys and sells shares for you, taking a small fee, because normal people cannot trade directly in the stock exchange on their own [12, 14].
 * **Jobber:** A dealer who buys and sells shares for himself to make a profit, but does not deal directly with normal investors [15].
 * **Bull (Tejiwala):** A trader who expects share prices to go *up* in the future, so they buy shares now to make a profit later [15].
 * **Bear (Mandiwala):** A trader who expects share prices to fall *down*, so they sell their shares quickly to avoid losing money [16].
 * **Rally:** When share prices keep moving upward for several days [13].
 * **Crash:** When share prices suddenly fall downward [13].
 * **Contract Note:** A special receipt or paper given by the broker that proves a buying or selling transaction actually happened [17].
 
 **SEBI - The Market Referee:**
 To make sure nobody cheats in this big market, there is a powerful referee called SEBI (Securities and Exchange Board of India) [18]. SEBI creates the rules, protects the people investing their money, and ensures that all buying and selling is completely fair and safe [19].

Introduction to Partnership and Partners.summary: Explains partnership features, the Partnership Act 1932, and preparing Final Accounts.
Introduction to Partnership and Partners.detailedSummary:
**Introduction to Partnership**
 Imagine you want to start a big lemonade stand, but you do not have enough money, time, or skills to do it all by yourself. You ask a friend to join you so you can share the costs, the hard work, and the risks [1]. When two or more people come together to run a business and share the money they make, it is called a "Partnership" [2]. The people doing this are individually called "Partners," and together they are known as a "Firm" [1].
 
 **Important Features of a Partnership**
 * **Agreement:** Partners make a plan or a rulebook together [3]. When this rulebook is written down on paper, it is called a "Partnership Deed" [3, 4].
 * **Number of People:** You need at least 2 people to start a partnership, and you can have a maximum of 50 partners [3].
 * **Sharing Money:** Partners agree on how to share the profits (the extra money earned) and losses (money lost) [5]. If they do not write down how to share it, they must share it equally [5, 6].
 * **Unlimited Liability:** This means if the business owes a lot of money to people and runs out of cash, the partners have to use their own personal money or property to pay off the debts [5]. 
 
 **The Indian Partnership Act, 1932**
 Sometimes, partners forget to write down important rules in their Partnership Deed [6]. When this happens, a special law from the government called the "Indian Partnership Act, 1932" acts as a referee [2, 6]. For example, if partners did not agree on whether someone should get a salary for doing extra work, this law steps in and says no partner gets a salary [7]. It provides rules to solve any missing issues or arguments [6].
 
 **Keeping Track of Money (Capital Accounts)**
 When partners bring cash or items into the business to help it run, it is called "Capital" [7]. The business keeps special notebooks to track how much money each partner put in or took out, which are called Capital Accounts [7]. They can track this in two ways: 
 1. **Fixed Capital Method:** The main starting money is kept in one account and never changes, while a second "Current Account" is used for everyday changes like profits or taking out cash [7-9]. 
 2. **Fluctuating Capital Method:** Everything is mixed into one single account, so the total amount goes up and down all the time [7, 10].
 
 **Partnership Final Accounts**
 At the very end of the year, partners want to know if their business was successful [11]. To find out, they prepare "Final Accounts," which act like an end-of-year report card [11, 12]. It has three main parts:
 1. **Trading Account:** This shows the basic money made directly from buying and selling things [11]. It tells them their "Gross Profit" or basic starting profit [11].
 2. **Profit and Loss Account:** This takes the basic profit and subtracts all the other business expenses (like paying for rent, electricity, or salaries) [11, 13]. This shows the "Net Profit," which is the actual final money the partners get to take home and share [11].
 3. **Balance Sheet:** This is not an account, but a special list showing everything the business owns on the last day of the year (Assets, like buildings, furniture, or cash) and everything the business owes to others (Liabilities, like bank loans) [11, 13, 14].

Accounts of Ã¢â¬ËNot for ProfitÃ¢â¬â.summary: Covers Receipts and Payments Accounts, Income and Expenditure Accounts, and Balance Sheets.
Accounts of Ã¢â¬ËNot for ProfitÃ¢â¬â.detailedSummary:
Imagine a club, a hospital, or a school [1]. Unlike a regular shop that wants to make money, these places just want to help people and provide services [1, 2]. These are called "Not for Profit" concerns [2]. Because their main goal is not to earn a profit, they do not make a normal "Profit and Loss Account" [3]. Instead, they use three special tools to keep track of their money:
 
 **1. Receipts and Payments Account**
 Think of this like a simple diary for a piggy bank [4]. It is a summary of all the cash that comes in and goes out [5]. It records every single coin that comes in (Receipts) and every single coin that goes out (Payments) [5, 6]. It does not matter if the cash is for last year, this year, or next yearÃ¢â¬âif it touches the piggy bank, it gets written down [6, 7]. It also does not matter if the money is for something small like daily bills (revenue) or something huge like buying a building (capital) [6]. It simply starts by showing how much cash they had at the beginning of the year and ends by showing exactly how much cash is left at the very end [5, 8].
 
 **2. Income and Expenditure Account**
 This account is a bit stricter and acts like a report card for *only the current year* [9, 10]. It only looks at the regular, day-to-day money earned (Income) and the regular, day-to-day money spent (Expenditure) [8, 9, 11]. It ignores money that belongs to the past or the future, and it ignores huge one-time purchases [9, 11]. If the organization earns more regular income than it spends, the extra money is called a "Surplus" [11]. If they spend more money than they earn, it is called a "Deficit" [11]. This surplus or deficit is then moved into their main savings fund [12]. 
 
 **3. Balance Sheet**
 This is like a photograph of everything the organization owns and owes on the very last day of the year [13]. On one side, it lists "Assets," which are all the valuable things they own, like their land, buildings, furniture, library books, and leftover cash [13, 14]. On the other side, it lists "Liabilities," which is the money they owe to others [13, 14]. Because there is no single business "owner" claiming the profits, they do not use a regular owner's capital account; instead, they have a "Capital Fund" [15, 16]. This fund is a big pool of money made up of special donations, entrance fees, and the "Surpluses" (savings) they collect over the years [15, 17].

Reconstitution of Partnership (Admission.summary: Details adjustments for goodwill, revaluation of assets, and new profit-sharing ratios.
Reconstitution of Partnership (Admission.detailedSummary:
**Admission of a Partner**
 When a business needs more money or new skills, the old partners can invite a new partner to join [1]. This creates a new agreement between everyone, which is called the reconstitution of the partnership [2]. The new partner brings in money (capital) and gets a right to share the future profits of the business [3].
 
 **New Profit-Sharing Ratios**
 To give the new partner a slice of the profit pie, the old partners must give up a piece of their own share [4]. The portion they give up is called the Sacrifice Ratio [5]. You calculate this by subtracting the new profit share from their old profit share [5]. To find the New Profit-Sharing Ratio for everyone, you pretend the total profit is exactly 1, subtract the new partner's share from it, and divide the remaining balance among the old partners [6].
 
 **Goodwill**
 Goodwill is the good name, fame, and reputation of a business that helps it easily earn more money [7]. Because the old partners worked hard in the past to build this good reputation, the new partner has to pay them for it [4]. 
 * **Premium Method:** The new partner brings their share of the goodwill in cash [8]. This cash is then distributed to the old partners based on how much profit they sacrificed [9].
 * **Valuation Method:** The new partner does not bring cash, so the old partners just calculate the goodwill's value and record it in the business's account books [10]. 
 Goodwill is usually calculated by looking at the average profits from previous years or by looking at the "super" or extra profits the business makes compared to normal businesses [11, 12].
 
 **Revaluation of Assets and Liabilities**
 Over time, the value of the firm's belongings (assets) and debts (liabilities) goes up and down [13]. Before the new partner officially joins, the business must update these prices to their current market value so the new partner doesn't unfairly gain or lose money from past events [13]. The business uses a special tool called a Revaluation Account to track these updates [14].
 * If an asset's value increases or a debt decreases, it is a profit [15].
 * If an asset's value decreases or a debt increases, it is a loss [15].
 Because all of these changes happened before the new friend joined, any final profit or loss from this process is given only to the old partners using their old profit-sharing agreement [14, 16, 17].

Reconstitution of Partnership (Retiremen.summary: Covers profit/loss and goodwill calculations when a partner leaves the firm.
Reconstitution of Partnership (Retiremen.detailedSummary:
Imagine you and two of your friends run a very successful lemonade stand together, and you all share the money you make. One day, one friend decides they want to stop working at the stand because they want to do something else. When a partner leaves a business like this, it is called the "Retirement of a Partner" [1, 2]. 
 
 When your friend leaves, you have to figure out exactly how much money belongs to them so they can take their fair share home. Here is how you calculate it in simple steps:
 
 **1. Splitting the Future Money (New Ratio and Gain Ratio)**
 Because your friend is leaving, they won't be taking a slice of the future profits anymore. This means you and your remaining friend will get a bigger slice of the pie from now on! The new way you split the money is called the "New Profit Sharing Ratio" [2]. The extra portion of the profits you are getting because your friend left is called your "Gain Ratio" [3, 4]. 
 
 **2. The Value of Good Fame (Goodwill)**
 Your lemonade stand is famous in the neighborhood, and lots of people buy from you because you are known for having the best lemonade. In business, this good reputation is very valuable and is called "Goodwill" [5, 6]. Since your retiring friend worked hard to help build that awesome reputation, it is only fair that you pay them for their share of this fame before they leave [5, 7]. 
 
 **3. Checking Your Stuff and Debts (Revaluation of Assets and Liabilities)**
 Before your friend leaves, you must check the true value of all the things the stand owns today, like the table, the pitchers, and the lemons (these are called Assets) [8]. You also check if the stand owes money to anyone (these are called Liabilities) [8]. If your items are worth more today than when you bought them, the stand makes a profit! You must give your retiring friend their fair piece of this profit because they owned these things with you [8].
 
 **4. Sharing the Piggy Bank (Reserves and Accumulated Profits)**
 If you and your friends had a piggy bank where you saved up some of your past profits for an emergency (called Reserve Funds), you have to open it up [9]. You must give the retiring friend their correct portion of those past savings, because they helped earn that money in the past [9, 10].
 
 **5. Saying Goodbye and Settling the Bill**
 Once you add up their original money, their share of the good fame (Goodwill), their share of the piggy bank (Reserves), and the profit from checking your stuff (Revaluation), you get the total amount you owe your retiring friend [1]. 
 You can pay them in a couple of ways:
 * Give them all the cash at once in a single lump sum [11].
 * If the stand doesn't have enough cash right now, you can turn the amount you owe them into a "Loan" [11]. This means you promise to pay them later, and you will give them a little extra money over time (called interest) as a thank you for waiting [11, 12].

Reconstitution of Partnership (Death of.summary: Explains settling accounts and calculating amounts due to a deceased partner's executor.
Reconstitution of Partnership (Death of.detailedSummary:
Imagine you and your friends are running a toy store together. This group of friends working together is called a partnership. Sometimes, a very sad thing happens, and one of the partners dies. In the business world, death is treated like a forced or "compulsory" retirement [1]. The toy store does not have to close forever; the remaining friends can agree to keep running the business [1]. 
 
 When a partner passes away, the business must give back the money that belonged to them. Because the partner is no longer there, this money is given to their family member or a legal helper. This person is called the "Executor," "Nominee," or "Administrator" [2]. 
 
 To figure out exactly how much money to give to the executor, the remaining friends must do some careful math [2, 3]. They need to add up all the things the deceased partner owned in the business. This total amount due includes:
 * **Capital:** The original money the partner put into the toy store [3].
 * **Savings:** The partner's share of any money the store saved up over the years, called general reserves or accumulated profits [2].
 * **Goodwill:** The partner's share of the store's good reputation and famous name [2].
 * **Earnings:** Any unpaid salary, or interest earned on their money [2]. 
 * **Current Profit:** The partner's share of the profit made from the beginning of the year exactly up to the day they passed away [2]. Since they don't know the exact profit for the whole year yet, they usually guess this amount by looking at the average profits from past years [4].
 
 The friends will also check the current value of the store's items, like tables and toys (Assets), and what the store owes (Liabilities) [3]. If the value went up, the extra profit is calculated and shared with the deceased partner's executor [2]. 
 
 Once the friends calculate the total money owed, they transfer it to a special "Executor's Loan Account" [2]. The business will safely pay this money to the family after all legal paperwork and rules are followed [2]. While the family waits to get paid, the business must add an extra 6% interest every year to the amount owed to ensure the family gets a fair deal [1]. 
 
 Finally, because one friend is no longer taking a slice of the profit pie, the remaining friends will get bigger slices in the future [5]. The extra share of profit the remaining friends now receive is called their "Gain" or "Benefit Ratio" [5]. This gain is simply calculated by taking their new bigger share and subtracting their old smaller share [3].

Dissolution of Partnership Firm.summary: Details the accounting treatment for closing a firm, including simple dissolution and insolvency.
Dissolution of Partnership Firm.detailedSummary:
**What is "Dissolution of a Partnership Firm"?**
 Imagine you and your friends opened a toy shop together. After a few years, you all decide you don't want to run the shop anymore. When you decide to close the business forever, sell everything, pay off everyone you owe, and go your separate ways, this is called the "Dissolution of a Partnership Firm" [1, 2]. 
 
 **Why do businesses close down?**
 There are a few simple reasons why friends might close their business [2]:
 * They simply agree to close it (Voluntary).
 * The specific time they agreed to run the business is over.
 * The business completely ran out of money (Insolvency).
 * The court or a judge orders them to close it.
 
 **How do they close the business? (Simple Dissolution)**
 When the business is closing, the friends can't just grab the money from the register and go home. They have to follow a fair, step-by-step process in their account books [3]:
 
 **Step 1: The "Realisation" Basket (Transfer Stage)**
 First, they take all the things the business owns, like tables, chairs, and leftover toys (these are called "Assets"). They also make a strict list of all the money they owe to other people, like the toy supplier or the bank (these are called "Liabilities"). They move all of these into a special, temporary account called the **"Realisation Account"** [3, 4].
 
 **Step 2: Selling and Paying (Disposal Stage)**
 Next, they sell all their toys, tables, and chairs to get cash [5]. With this cash, they start paying back the people they owe money to [6]. The rules say they must pay outside people first (like the bank or the suppliers) before they are allowed to keep any money for themselves [7, 8]. 
 
 **Step 3: Figuring out the leftovers (Distribution Stage)**
 After selling everything and paying the outside bills, they look at their Realisation Account. If they made more money from selling things than they expected, it's a "Profit." If they didn't get enough money, it's a "Loss" [3]. The friends share this profit or loss among themselves [9, 10]. Finally, whatever cash is left in the business's piggy bank is fairly divided among the friends, and the firm's books are closed for good [8, 11].
 
 **What happens if they run out of money completely? (Insolvency)**
 Sometimes, a business has a lot of bad luck and owes more money than it has. When someone cannot pay their debts at all, it is called "Insolvency" [12].
 
 * **When one friend becomes insolvent:** Imagine one friend doesn't have enough money in their own piggy bank at home to pay their fair share of the business's loss. That friend is "insolvent" [13, 14]. Because the business was a team effort, the other friends (the "solvent" ones who still have money) must step up and pay off their friend's missing share out of their own pockets [14, 15].
 * **When all friends become insolvent:** What if *all* the friends are totally out of money and their personal piggy banks are empty? In this sad situation, they gather whatever little cash the business has left and pay off the people they owe as fairly as they can [15]. The people they owe just have to accept that they won't get all their money back. The unpaid bills go into a special "Deficiency Account," which basically means "the money we are missing and can never pay," and the business is officially closed [15, 16].

Bills of Exchange.summary: Covers the meaning, drafting, retaining, discounting, and dishonouring of bills.
Bills of Exchange.detailedSummary:
Imagine you sell a bicycle to your friend, but your friend does not have the money to pay you right now [1]. To make sure you get paid later, you write down a special "I Owe You" note, which in business is called a **Bill of Exchange** [2]. This is a written paper where someone makes a strict promise to pay a specific amount of money on a certain future date [2]. The person who writes this note and expects to get the money is called the **Drawer** [3]. The person who buys the item and promises to pay the money is called the **Drawee** [4]. 
 
 **Drafting the Bill**
 Drafting is the process of creating this written note [5]. You cannot just write it quickly on a scrap of paper; it must have specific details to be official. It needs the date, the exact amount of money in numbers and words, a proper government stamp, and the names of the people involved [6, 7]. At first, this piece of paper is just called a "draft" [5]. It only becomes a real, powerful Bill of Exchange once your friend (the Drawee) reads it, agrees to the rules, and signs their name under the word "Accepted" [5].
 
 **Retaining the Bill**
 Retaining simply means keeping the signed note safe [8]. You hold onto the paper and wait patiently until the promised payment date arrives, which is called the "due date" [8, 9]. On that exact day, you take the note to your friend, show it to them, and they hand you your money [8, 10]. 
 
 **Discounting the Bill**
 What if you cannot wait for the due date because you desperately need the money right now? You can use a feature called "discounting" [11]. You can take your signed Bill of Exchange to your bank [11]. The bank will take the note from you and give you cash early [11]. However, the bank will keep a tiny piece of the money as a fee for doing you a favor, which is called the "discount" [11]. Later, when the due date finally arrives, the bank will go to your friend and collect the full amount of money [11].
 
 **Dishonouring the Bill**
 Sometimes, the promised payment date arrives, but when you (or the bank) ask your friend for the money, they say they cannot pay [10, 12]. When a person fails to pay the money on the promised date, the promise is broken, and this is called "dishonouring" the bill [10]. Because a broken promise is a serious problem in business, you can take the bill to a legal officer called a **Notary Public** [12, 13]. This officer will officially record and stamp the fact that your friend refused to pay, which is called "noting" the dishonour [12, 14].

Company Accounts - Issue of Shares.summary: Explains accounting for shares issued at par, premium, discount, and handling over/under subscription.
Company Accounts - Issue of Shares.detailedSummary:
Imagine you want to build a giant toy factory, but it costs a huge amount of money. You cannot pay for it all by yourself, so you invite many people to give a small amount of money [1, 2]. In return, you give them a tiny piece of ownership in your factory. This tiny piece of ownership is called a "Share," and all the money you collect together is called "Share Capital" [2]. 
 
 **Types of Shares**
 There are two main types of shares you can give people:
 * **Equity Shares:** These are the regular owners. They take the biggest risk, but if the factory does really well, they get the most reward (profit). They get their share of the profit only after everyone else is paid [3].
 * **Preference Shares:** These are like VIP owners. They have a special "preference" or priority. They always get a fixed amount of profit before the Equity shareholders get anything, and if the factory ever closes, they get their money back first [4].
 
 **How to Sell Shares to the Public**
 When your company wants to sell shares, it does not ask for all the money at once. It happens in steps:
 1. **Prospectus:** You send out a big invitation telling everyone about your great factory and inviting them to buy shares [5].
 2. **Application:** People who want to buy shares send you an "Application" along with a small piece of the money [6].
 3. **Allotment:** You accept their applications and officially give them the shares. They pay you another piece of the money called "Allotment money" [7]. 
 4. **Calls:** If they still owe you money for the share, you can "call" them later to ask for the remaining balance (First Call, Second Call, etc.) [8].
 
 **Selling at Par, Premium, or Discount**
 Every share has a real price printed on it, called the "Face Value" (let's say it is Ã¢âÂ¹10).
 * **At Par:** If you sell the share for exactly Ã¢âÂ¹10, it is called issuing "at par" [9].
 * **At Premium:** If your toy factory is super famous, everyone will want to be an owner! You can sell your Ã¢âÂ¹10 share for a higher price, like Ã¢âÂ¹13. That extra Ã¢âÂ¹3 is called a "Premium" [10]. This extra money is profit and is kept safely in a special account called the Share Premium Account [10].
 * **At Discount:** If you sell your Ã¢âÂ¹10 share for a cheaper price, like Ã¢âÂ¹9, the Ã¢âÂ¹1 less is called a "Discount" [11]. However, the rules of the government (Companies Act 2013) now say that companies generally cannot sell shares to the public at a discount because it causes a loss to the company [11, 12].
 
 **Over-Subscription and Under-Subscription**
 Sometimes, the number of people who want to buy shares does not perfectly match the number of shares you have.
 * **Over-Subscription:** Imagine you only have 10,000 shares to sell, but people send applications to buy 15,000 shares! This means you have too many buyers [13]. Since you only have 10,000 shares, you can either reject the extra 5,000 applications and give them their money back [13], or you can do a "Partial Allotment" (also called Pro-Rata), meaning you give people slightly fewer shares than they asked for and use their extra money to pay for their future "Calls" [9, 14].
 * **Under-Subscription:** Imagine you want to sell 10,000 shares, but you only get applications for 8,000 shares. This means you have fewer buyers than expected [15]. You simply accept the 8,000 applications, give them their shares, and only collect money for those 8,000 shares [15].
