Because his own children ignore him, the father is forced to be all alone and feels very sad and isolated in his own home [1]. 
 
 Since he cannot find love and connection with his family in the real world, he goes to sleep listening to the noisy static on the radio [3]. To feel better, he starts dreaming about his past ancestors and his future grandchildren [3]. The poem shows us how important love, concern, and family values are by giving us a heartbreaking example of a father who is deeply hurt by his children's bad behavior and lack of care [1, 6].

Money.summary: Discusses happiness, fulfillment, and the futility of materialism.
Money.detailedSummary:
The poem "Money" by William H. Davies tells a very simple but powerful story about what it means to be truly happy [1, 2]. It teaches us a big lesson: having a lot of money and buying a lot of things will not make you happy inside [2, 3].
 
 When the poet was a very rich man, he had many people around him who called themselves his "friends" [4]. They came knocking on his door all day long [4]. But the poet soon realized that these people were fake [4]. They did not actually care about him; they only cared about his money [1, 4]. Because he was surrounded by fake people, the poet felt like he had to hide his true feelings [4]. He compared himself to a little child who is holding a fun toy trumpet but is told he is not allowed to blow it [4]. He had everything he could buy, but he felt trapped and sad [4].
 
 Then, the poet started watching poor people [4]. He noticed something amazing. Even though poor men do not have fancy things, their hearts are light and free [4]. They work very hard from morning until night, but they still laugh, and their families are cheerful, humming happily like bees [4]. The poet saw that rich people often frown and look worried, while poor people smile and enjoy the simple things in life [4]. 
 
 At the end of the story, the poet loses his wealth and becomes poor [1, 2]. But instead of being sad, he is finally happy! [2]. Without his money, all the fake friends ran away [1]. Now, he only has a very small number of friends left, but they are real, true friends who love him for who he is [1, 2]. 
 
 The main message of this poem is that chasing after money and toys is useless if you want to be happy [3]. True joy comes from living a simple life and having real friends, which is something that money can never buy [1, 2].

She Walks in Beauty.summary: Highlights inner beauty, platonic love, and sensitivity.
She Walks in Beauty.detailedSummary:
The poem "She Walks in Beauty" by Lord Byron is about a woman who is incredibly beautiful both on the outside and the inside [1]. 
 
 First, the poet talks about how she looks. He compares her beautiful walk to a clear night sky filled with bright stars [1, 2]. He says her face and her eyes have the absolute perfect mix of dark and bright colors [2]. This makes a soft and gentle light around her, which is much nicer and softer than the harsh, glaring light of the daytime [2]. 
 
 Next, he explains that her appearance is so perfectly balanced that if you added just one more dark shadow or took away one single ray of light, it would ruin her special beauty [2]. But then, the poet starts talking about what is inside her head. He says her calm and sweet face proves that her thoughts are very pure, happy, and peaceful [2]. Her mind is a lovely, safe home for these good thoughts [2].
 
 Finally, the poet looks at her soft brow, her glowing cheeks, and her gentle smile [2]. He says her sweet smile tells a story about how she lives her life. It shows everyone that she spends her days doing good and kind things [3]. Because she is so good, she has a mind that is completely at peace with the world, and a heart that is full of innocent, pure love [3]. 
 
 The main lesson of the poem is that true beauty is not just about having a pretty face, but about having a good heart and a kind soul [1, 3]. The poet admires her completely, but it is a very respectful and pure kind of love, showing great sensitivity to her inner goodness [1, 4].

Small Towns and Rivers.summary: Connects eternity, divinity, and concern for the environment.
Small Towns and Rivers.detailedSummary:
The poem "Small Towns and Rivers" by Mamang Dai is about her small hometown located near a beautiful river [1, 2]. In this poem, the poet talks about three big ideas: caring for nature, things that last forever (eternity), and God or holy spirits (divinity) [1]. 
 
 First, the poet shows a deep concern for the environment [1]. She says that looking at the small towns actually reminds her of death [2]. This is because as the towns grow bigger, people destroy the beautiful trees and nature to build them [1]. She feels sad and anxious about the future because she knows that harming nature is like bringing death to the earth [1, 3].
 
 Second, the poet explains the idea of eternity, which means something that lives on and never dies [1]. She explains that human life is very short, and people eventually pass away, leaving behind only sad rituals [1, 2]. However, the river is eternal and immortal [1]. She writes that "the river has a soul" and knows the "immortality of water" [3, 4]. This means the river will keep flowing forever, from the top of the mountains down to the dry earth, long after humans are gone [4].
 
 Finally, the poem connects to divinity, which means a connection to God [1, 3]. The people in the poet's town believe that when someone dies, their soul stays alive inside the trees, rivers, and nature around them [1]. When people pass away, they are placed pointing to the west so their souls can rise up and walk into the "golden east" where the sun lives [3]. Because they live so closely with the immortal river and nature, the people feel very close to heaven and simply want to "walk with the gods" [3].

Summary Writing.summary: Teaches skimming, scanning, and condensing information.
Summary Writing.detailedSummary:
A summary is like telling a friend about a long movie in just a few minutes by only sharing the most important parts [1]. To write a good summary, you need to learn how to skim, scan, and condense information [2]. 
 
 Skimming and scanning mean reading a passage quickly to find the main ideas and important words, just like searching for your favorite toy in a big toy box [2]. Condensing means making the text shorter by taking out extra words you do not need so that only the main message is left [1, 2].
 
 Here are the simple steps to write a summary:
 1. Read the text two times carefully so you really understand it [3, 4].
 2. Ask yourself, "What is this story mostly about?" to find the big idea [3, 4].
 3. Write your first draft using short, simple sentences of your own [3, 4].
 4. Connect your short sentences together smoothly [3].
 5. Take out small details and extra examples, and never add your own personal opinions [1, 3].
 6. Read your draft again, fix any mistakes, and write your final copy [4].
 
 You can make your sentences shorter and condense information using these easy tricks:
 * Take out repeating words, like changing "circle around" to just "circle" [5].
 * Remove extra describing words, like changing "I totally agree" to just "I agree" [6].
 * Use one word instead of a whole group of words, like saying "autobiography" instead of "a story about a person written by themselves" [7].

Do Schools Really Kill Creativity? (Mind.summary: Focuses on mind-mapping frames, generating ideas, and clarity of thought.
Do Schools Really Kill Creativity? (Mind.detailedSummary:
**What is Mind-Mapping?**
 Mind-Mapping is like drawing a map or a picture of your thoughts. When you have a lot of thoughts mixed up in your head, a mind map helps you organize them so everything becomes clear. 
 
 Imagine your thoughts are like a big tree:
 * **The Trunk (Main Idea):** You draw a circle in the middle of a blank page. This is your main topic or the big idea you want to think about.
 * **The Thick Branches (Supporting Ideas):** From that center circle, you draw lines going outward. These are the big ideas connected to your main topic. 
 * **The Small Twigs (Extra Details):** From the thick branches, you draw smaller lines. These are the tiny details or examples.
 
 Instead of writing long, boring paragraphs, you use balloons, arrows, keywords, and colors. 
 
 **Why is Mind-Mapping Helpful?**
 * **It generates new ideas (Brainstorming):** It lets your thoughts flow freely without worrying about perfect sentences.
 * **It gives clarity of thought:** Because you can see the "big picture" all at once, you understand how different ideas connect to each other.
 * **It makes learning easy:** Our brains remember pictures and shapes much better than plain words. It is a great tool for taking notes, writing essays, or studying for exams.
 
 **Do Schools Really Kill Creativity?**
 The chapter also talks about a big debate: Do schools stop children from being creative?
 
 **The First View: Yes, they sometimes do.**
 An expert named Sir Ken Robinson believes that schools often push children out of their natural creativity. He tells a true story about a little 8-year-old girl named Gillian. Gillian could never sit still in class. Her school thought she was a "problem student" who had a learning difficulty. But a smart doctor watched her and told her mother, "Gillian is not sick; she is a dancer." Her mother took her to a dance school. Gillian grew up to be one of the most famous dance creators in the world! If her school had forced her to just sit still, her amazing creative talent might have been killed.
 
 **The Second View: No, schools build creativity.**
 Another expert named Julian Astle says that schools actually *help* creativity. He explains that to be truly creative, you first need basic knowledgeÃ¢â¬âlike reading, writing, and understanding facts. Schools provide this strong foundation. For example, you cannot be a creative writer if you do not know how to spell, and you cannot be creative in math if you do not know your numbers. 
 
 **The Lesson:**
 Creativity is just as important as reading and writing. Whether in math, art, or history, we need a clear mind to find creative solutions, and tools like Mind-Mapping help us bring that creativity to life!

Note-Making.detailedSummary:
**Note-Making: A Simple Guide**
 
 Note-making is like creating a personalized, easy-to-read map of your lesson [1, 2]. It is an active and focused skill where you connect different ideas together using simple methods like bullet points, tables, or tree diagrams [3]. 
 
 **How It Helps You Understand Better (Reading Comprehension)**
 When you make notes, you do not just blindly copy what a teacher says or what a book writes [1, 4]. Instead, you gather information, think about it deeply, and put it into your own words [2, 4]. This forces your brain to truly understand the topic before you write it down, giving you "more understanding" of the subject [4]. It also helps you see how different points link to one another clearly [4]. 
 
 **How It Helps You Keep Things Short and Exact (Preciseness)**
 Instead of writing long, full sentences, note-making teaches you to pull out only the most important keys and facts [4]. You write these down in short forms [4]. This makes your notes precise, meaning they only contain exactly what you need without any extra, confusing words.
 
 **Why It Is Useful**
 * It makes remembering facts much easier because you are using your reading and listening senses together [1].
 * It helps you concentrate and focus better on your studies [1].
 * It gives you a quick, simple reference guide that you built yourself, which is perfect for studying right before an exam [1, 2].

Statement of Purpose.summary: Guides the concise, organized presentation of facts and personal vision.
Statement of Purpose.detailedSummary:
A **Statement of Purpose (SOP)** is like a special, personal letter you write when you want to join a new school or university [1]. It is an essay that tells the story of who you are, what your big dreams are, and what makes you unique [1]. 
 
 Think of it as a way to neatly and clearly present the facts about your life and your personal vision for the future [2]. 
 
 Here is what it does in simple words:
 * **It introduces you:** It shows your real personality and the good qualities you have grown over time [1].
 * **It explains your choices:** It tells the school exactly why you want to study a certain subject, why you chose their school, and how you plan to reach your goals [1].
 * **It acts as a key:** It is the gateway to your future career because teachers read it to decide if you are a good match for their school [1].
 
 **How to write it step-by-step:**
 An SOP should be around 500 words long and divided into 5 or 6 clear paragraphs [3].
 * **Paragraph 1:** Introduce yourself, your background, and your main goal in life [3].
 * **Paragraph 2:** Explain why you really want to join this specific course [3].
 * **Paragraph 3:** Talk about your special skills, hobbies, and activities that make you perfect for this goal [4]. 
 * **Paragraph 4:** Share any past experiences, projects, or practice you have already done in this subject [4].
 * **Paragraph 5 (Ending):** Finish by explaining how joining their school will help you build a great career in the long run [5].
 
 **Important rules:** 
 Always write in a clear, easy-to-understand way, making sure your facts are organized [2, 5]. Do not make spelling mistakes, and be very confident so the readers believe in your dreams [5].

Drafting a Virtual Message.summary: Enhances creativity, imagination, and effective communication.
Drafting a Virtual Message.detailedSummary:
"Drafting a Virtual Message" is about learning how to share your thoughts using computers, the internet, or mobile phones when you cannot talk to someone face-to-face [1]. 
 
 **What is a Virtual Message?**
 Imagine you need to tell someone something very important, but they are not with you in the same room. You use a screen to send them a quick, written note [1]. This is called a virtual message.
 
 **How does it help your brain?**
 Writing these short messages is a great brain exercise! It helps you grow in three big ways [2]:
 * **Creativity:** You learn how to create a neat, perfectly sized note out of a long conversation [2, 3]. 
 * **Imagination:** You have to picture in your mind how the other person will read and feel about your words since they cannot see your face or hear your voice [2].
 * **Effective Communication:** This means learning how to be super clear. You practice telling people exactly what they need to know so they do not get confused [2].
 
 **How do you write a good message?**
 Think of it like building a simple tower with blocks. You just need the right pieces in the right order [4]:
 1. **Date and Time:** Write down when you are sending the note [4].
 2. **Name:** Who is the note for? [4].
 3. **The Message Body:** This is the most important block! Only write the true, necessary facts [5]. Do not add extra, new, or unnecessary stories [5]. Keep it very short and simple.
 4. **Your Name:** Who is sending it? Write your name at the bottom [4].
 
 **Simple Rules to Remember:**
 * Use simple words and correct grammar [5].
 * Be polite, respectful, and friendly [5].
 * Do not use tricky words, abbreviations, or jokes that might confuse the person reading it [5].
 * Always double-check your message before you send it to make sure it is perfect [5]. 
 
 By practicing these steps, you use your creative mind to share exact ideas quickly and clearly!

Group Discussion.summary: Builds leadership skills, problem-solving, and mutual understanding.
Group Discussion.detailedSummary:
A group discussion is when a small group of people sit together to talk about one specific topic and share their ideas [1]. In your English book, learning how to write and participate in group discussions helps you learn three very important life skills [2]:
 
 **1. Building Leadership Skills:** Being a leader in a group does not mean being bossy. Instead, it means you help start the conversation, make sure everyone gets a fair turn to speak, and gently guide the group so nobody gets off-topic [1, 3]. It teaches you how to help a team work well together to reach a goal [1].
 
 **2. Problem-Solving:** Sometimes, a group is given a tricky situation or a case study to talk about [1]. Because everyone has different thoughts, the group can look at the problem from many different angles [1]. By sharing ideas, the team can figure out the best way to fix the problem together [1, 4]. It is like putting together a puzzle where everyone holds a different piece.
 
 **3. Mutual Understanding:** This means learning to be a kind friend and a good listener [3, 4]. In a discussion, people will have different opinions and views [5]. Mutual understanding teaches you to listen carefully to your peers, respect what others say, politely agree or disagree, and work together peacefully to reach a conclusion that everyone is happy with [3, 4, 6].

History of Novel.summary: Connects the past and present through understanding the evolution of the novel.
History of Novel.detailedSummary:
A "novel" is simply a very long story written in normal, everyday language, which helps us understand human life and the society of the time [1]. The word "novel" actually comes from an old Italian word, "novella," which means "new" [1]. 
 
 **The Beginning of the Journey (The Past)**
 The idea of writing long stories goes back about 2,000 years, starting with old Greek adventure tales that were full of magic and ideal love [2]. The world's very first real novel is considered to be the 'Tale of Genji', written in the year 1010 [3]. Later, in Europe, the novel really began with a famous Spanish book called 'Don Quixote' [3]. 
 
 **The Big Change for Common People**
 A long time ago, only very rich people read books, and they liked stories about grand tragedies or magical romances [3]. However, around the 18th century (the 1700s), factories started being built, and a new group of normal, working peopleÃ¢â¬âcalled the "middle class"Ã¢â¬âsuddenly had a little more money and time to read [3, 4]. These common people did not want to read about fantasy kings and queens; they wanted stories that felt real and showed everyday life and actual problems [3]. Because of this, the novel became incredibly popular as a way to give a voice to the dreams and struggles of ordinary people [4]. 
 
 **Growing into the Present**
 As time passed, storytelling grew and changed. Women writers made huge contributions to this journey; for example, Mary Shelley wrote *Frankenstein* (an early science fiction story), and Agatha Christie created brilliant detective mysteries [5, 6]. Indian writers also joined the journey, starting with the first Indian English novel, 'RajmohanÃ¢â¬â¢s Wife', and eventually writing about important social and national issues [7]. By the 20th century, writers started using novels to talk about modern politics, psychology, and deep human thoughts [8]. 
 
 **How a Novel is Built**
 To connect all these ideas from the past to the present, every novel uses six simple building blocks [9]:
 1. **Theme:** The main message or central idea of the story [9].
 2. **Plot:** The actual events and actions that happen in the story [10].
 3. **Character:** The people (or heroes and villains) acting out the story [10].
 4. **Setting:** The place, time, and weather where the story happens [11].
 5. **Conflict:** The big problem or struggle the characters have to face [11].
 6. **Language/Style:** The specific words and tricks the author uses to make the story exciting [11].
 
 **Types of Novels Today**
 Because the novel has evolved so much over history, today we have many fun types of stories to choose from [12]. There are "Historical" novels about the past, "Gothic" novels full of spooky haunted buildings, "Autobiographical" novels about a real person's life, "Science Fiction" novels about space and the future, and "Detective" novels where a clever person solves a crime [6, 13-15]. 
 
 By looking at the history of the novel, we can clearly see how stories started as simple magical tales in the past and slowly grew into a powerful way to understand our complex, modern world today [2, 8, 16].

To Sir, with Love.summary: Deals with problem-solving, sympathy, and handling troubles through an autobiographical lens.
To Sir, with Love.detailedSummary:
"To Sir, with Love" is a true story based on the life of the writer, E. R. Braithwaite [1]. The writer was a very smart engineer who served in the war, but he could not find a regular job just because he was a black man [1]. To earn money, he took a job as a teacher in a very rough school in London [2]. 
 
 The students in his class were famous for being troublemakers who were thrown out of other schools [2]. They were very disrespectful, used bad words, slammed their desks, and bullied him [3]. At first, the teacher got very angry and scolded them, but he quickly realized that getting angry did not solve the problem [3, 4]. 
 
 To solve this trouble, he decided to use a very different and clever idea: he treated these naughty students like respectful grown-ups [4]. He made a rule that they must call him "Sir" and the girls must be called "Miss" [4]. He also took them on fun field trips to museums and theaters to show them the world outside [5]. 
 
 Even when the students made mistakes and hurt his feelings, the teacher chose to forgive them [5]. He showed them great sympathy, patience, and love [5, 6]. Because he treated them so nicely, the students began to change their bad habits [4]. They became clean, polite, and caring towards each other [4]. 
 
 In a special school event described in the book, these once-naughty students confidently give reports to the whole school [7]. They talk smartly about the brotherhood of mankind and how all people in the world depend on each other, no matter their color or race [8]. The story beautifully shows that by using patience, respect, and sympathy, you can solve the biggest problems and change people for the better [6].

Around the World in Eighty Days.summary: An adventure touring the world, meeting different people, and exchanging ideas.
Around the World in Eighty Days.detailedSummary:
**The Main Characters**
 Meet Phileas Fogg. He is a very strict and lonely man who lives in England [1, 2]. He has a loyal and funny helper named Passepartout [2]. 
 
 **The Big Bet**
 One day, Mr. Fogg makes a big bet with his friends at his club [1]. He promises he can travel all the way around the entire world in just 80 days [1, 3]. His friends think this is impossible because so many things can go wrong, but they agree to bet 20,000 pounds [1]. 
 
 **The Great Adventure**
 Mr. Fogg and his helper pack their bags and quickly leave. They use many different ways to travel, like trains, ships, and they even ride on an elephant [4]. Because they travel to so many countries, they meet very different people and see many new places [3, 5]. During their trip, they save a beautiful lady named Aouda from danger, and she joins them on their adventure [2, 6]. 
 
 **The Bad Guy**
 There is a big problem on their trip. A police detective named Fix secretly follows them everywhere [7]. Fix wrongly thinks Mr. Fogg is a bad man who robbed a bank in England [8]. Fix keeps trying to put obstacles in their way [7]. Right near the very end of the trip, Fix finally arrests Mr. Fogg and puts him in jail [8, 9].
 
 **The Sad Moment**
 Soon, Fix finds out the real bank robber was already caught, so he lets Mr. Fogg out of jail [9]. But because Mr. Fogg was stuck in jail, he arrives in London late and thinks he has lost the big bet [8]. He goes home feeling very sad because he thinks he has lost all his money and his honor [9, 10]. However, Aouda tells Mr. Fogg that she loves him and asks to marry him [11, 12]. This makes Mr. Fogg very happy again [4].
 
 **The Big Surprise**
 Mr. Fogg sends his helper, Passepartout, to find a priest for their wedding [11]. When Passepartout goes outside, he discovers a huge secret! It is actually Saturday, not Sunday [13]. They had arrived one whole day early [13]! Because they traveled all the way around the world towards the sun (to the East), they had accidentally gained an extra day without even knowing it [14, 15]. 
 
 **The Happy Ending**
 Passepartout runs back, grabs Mr. Fogg, and they rush to the club as fast as they can [13, 14]. Mr. Fogg walks through the doors with only seconds left and wins the bet [14, 16]! In the end, Mr. Fogg does not care much about the money he won [17, 18]. He is just happy because the amazing adventure around the world gave him good friends and a wonderful wife [2, 4].

The Sign of Four.summary: Explores mystery, suspense, and detective work.
The Sign of Four.detailedSummary:
"The Sign of Four" is a very exciting detective story about a famous and smart detective named Sherlock Holmes and his good friend, Dr. Watson [1]. 
 
 One day, a nice young lady named Mary Morstan comes to them for help. She is very sad because her father went missing ten years ago, and she never found out what happened to him. Also, for the past six years, a secret person has been sending her a beautiful, shiny pearl in the mail every single year [1-3]! Now, this secret person has sent a letter asking to meet her. 
 
 Sherlock Holmes and Dr. Watson go with Mary to meet the secret person. His name is Thaddeus Sholto [1, 4]. Thaddeus tells them a giant secret. MaryÃ¢â¬â¢s father and ThaddeusÃ¢â¬â¢s father were friends in the army in India, and they had a giant, sparkling treasure [5]. Sadly, MaryÃ¢â¬â¢s father died by accident while they were looking at the treasure. ThaddeusÃ¢â¬â¢s father got scared and hid the treasure away [5]. Later, before Thaddeus's father died, he was very afraid of a man with a wooden leg. When he died, a note was left on him that said "The Sign of Four" [4, 5].
 
 Thaddeus tells them that his brother, Bartholomew, finally found where the treasure was hidden inside their big house [6]. But when Sherlock, Watson, Mary, and Thaddeus go to look, they find something terrible. Bartholomew is dead because someone hit him with a poisoned dart, and the giant treasure box is completely gone [7, 8]!
 
 Sherlock Holmes starts looking for clues like footprints [9]. He figures out that two people took the treasure: a man with a wooden leg and a very small man [7, 8]. Sherlock uses a special tracking dog and his smart brain to find out the bad guys are escaping on a boat down the river [8, 9].
 
 Sherlock and Watson get on a police boat and chase the bad guys down the river in an exciting race [10]! The small man tries to shoot them with a dart, but Sherlock and Watson stop him. The bad guys' boat crashes into the mud, and they catch the man with the wooden leg, whose name is Jonathan Small [10].
 
 Jonathan Small tells them the whole story. Long ago in India, he and three friends stole the treasure, which is why they called themselves "The Four" [10, 11]. They were caught and put in an island prison. Small made a promise to give some treasure to the prison guards (Mary's dad and Thaddeus's dad) if they helped him escape [10-12]. But Thaddeus's dad took all the treasure for himself and ran away, breaking his promise [11, 12]! Small was very angry and spent years trying to escape to get his treasure back [11-13]. 
 
 When Jonathan Small is caught, the treasure box is empty. He says that during the boat chase, he threw all the sparkling jewels deep into the river so no one else could ever have them [8, 14]. Because the treasure is in the river, Mary is not going to be super rich. But this turns out to be a good thing! Dr. Watson really liked Mary, but he was afraid to ask a rich lady to marry him. Now that the treasure is gone, he bravely asks Mary to marry him, and she happily says yes [14-16]!
